author: m.kalinin
content: Thanks for raising this <@402080271563751435> . One of my concerns of this EIP was related to the range sync in zk era, where no EL is required. I believe a proof with public input will have to be pulled off by CL for each payload or beacon block to verify EL STF (and both layers STF in a more distant future)

author: m.kalinin
content: If the above makes sense then stepping away from payloads range sync today is the move towards the opposite direction. I believe `ProofEngine` won't be (and shall not be) equipped with a sophisticated network layer that would handle payload sync as EL is able to today

author: taulepton_
content:
Yes that’s correct. The proof engine has no external network to sync payloads and as such depends on the CL to provide payloads. 

Just a few additional comments (thinking out loud):
- if we want to support VOPS node using the proof engine to drive forkchoice such that we can maintain a transaction pool for FOCIL then we may need to connect to devp2p network. However, this is far down the line and not something we have concluded on/can count on to mitigate the impact of deprecation of dangerous sync.
- After EIP8142 the proof engine will no longer require execution payloads as it can guarantee payload availability via sampling of blocks.

author: m.kalinin
content: but it will anyway require some information for each payload right?

author: taulepton_
content: Thinking about this proposal I think it may actually be redundant. There already exists a commitment which fits the description described in this EIP, that is the new_payload_request_root, e.g. the ssz tree hash root of the new payload body type used in the engine api. Furthermore the engine API already has checks to ensure that the execution payload is consistent with the parent beacon root.

author: taulepton_
content: We are using the new_payload_request_root in our zkEVM guest programs

author: taulepton_
content: Yes correct, however it will only require the payload header - not the payload body

author: taulepton_
content: <@755590043632140352> I would recommend we do a review of the new_payload_request_root and also the consistency checks the engine API does against the parent beacon block root. It may be that there is already enough coverage that allow us to not check the chain prefix comprehensively.

author: m.kalinin
content: This will require changes to the Engine API, right?

author: taulepton_
content: No the engine api already does these checks

author: taulepton_
content: There is specifically a check against the parent beacon block. The parent beacon block is already an accumulator of the execution requests etc

author: m.kalinin
content: hmmm, it does the `block_hash` verification since the merge, there is no SSZ hash_tree_root checks in the Engine API

author: m.kalinin
content: and parent_beacon_block_root is a part of the `block_hash` check

author: taulepton_
content: Right so doesn’t that check already satisfy our requirements here?

author: taulepton_
content: The new_payload_request_root isn’t materialised in the standard CL protocol. We will be making it first class to support BIB / payload sapling for zk attesters

author: m.kalinin
content: it does, but it requires the full payload to be passed to the EL, this EIP proposes to get rid of the need for CL to pull full payloads

author: taulepton_
content: Get rid of CL -> EL payloads entirely or only for range sync?

author: m.kalinin
content: for range sync of the finalized prefix of the chain

author: taulepton_
content: I think that’s fine then. In any case most EL’s already do their own isolated sync of the finalized prefix

author: m.kalinin
content: maybe in the future it would be enough to verify the finalized payload via ProofEngine to deem the whole prefix valid?

author: taulepton_
content: Yes I think that’s is already in place

author: m.kalinin
content: then the sync in the future would look like as the following: verify proofs for each payload starting from the finalized one and that's it

author: taulepton_
content: The accumulator exists in the beacon block and the engine API acts on the latest block. Verifying the latest block using the engine API also gives guarantees via the block hash check which is sensitive to the beacon block root?

author: taulepton_
content: Yep that’s how it’s currently implemented in light house for EIP8025

author: m.kalinin
content: the thing is that we have to check each payload independently, specifically check that the payload fields that matter to CL has same values in CL and EL. this is why we have to introduce an accumulator of those fields. Will proof verification give us that property? I guess so because one of the inputs into proof verification is the beacon block root

author: taulepton_
content: Isn’t the beacon block is already the accumulator

author: taulepton_
content: Are you expecting that EL’s are currently checking this for the finalised chain prefix because I believe currently they do not.

author: m.kalinin
content: hmm, that's true. actually, this EIP might not be needed 😄

author: m.kalinin
content: you're right, `parent_beacon_block_root` commits to all historical beacon block data -- this is the accumulator we need, and if the `block_hash` verification with that root passes we can say that EL and CL have the same values for the whole prefix of the chain

author: taulepton_
content: Yep agreed

author: nero_eth
content: hmm I don't think it fully gets us there: parent_beacon_block_root sits behind ssz htr on the CL side and keccak/rlp on the EL side so neither layer can compute the same commitment independently, and the EL doesn't currently verify payload.withdrawals_root against state.latest_withdrawals_root anyway, so e.g. a withdrawal substitution attack still slips through.

author: nero_eth
content: The example I'm thinking of is an EL payload containing a different withdrawal than what the CL block commits to...

author: m.kalinin
content: full `block_hash` verification implies the CL and EL agrees on the content of the parent beacon block root and parent payload, and all the ancestor blocks. Then it also implies the same for the current beacon block root and the current payload

author: taulepton_
content: I’m afk this moment but if someone could provide spec url for the block hash verification on the engine that would provide good context for this

author: taulepton_
content: The idea is they don’t compute the same commitment. The CL provides the beacon root and the EL takes that as input in the engine. I need to review the actual spec in more detail to assess the withdrawals concern

author: nero_eth
content: my understanding is that EIP-8237 is about the [S, F] range where the proof engine doesn't run

author: nero_eth
content: there's no spec yet, afaict. This thread and the EIPs PR is all there is right now

author: taulepton_
content: There already exists checks in the engine against parent beacon block

author: taulepton_
content: The EL already does a decoupled sync for the finalized prefix

author: nero_eth
content: Where is "there" now?

author: taulepton_
content: In clients

author: taulepton_
content: [S,F] is s the snapshot?

author: nero_eth
content: Right but how would you prevent, in the [S, F] range, that e.g. the withdrawals are the same at each layer, EL and CL, and there's not a case where both layers believe they have a valid finalized chain where, in truth, the EL chain builds on top of an invalid chain?

author: nero_eth
content: No S here is just some block before finality.

author: taulepton_
content: The beacon block already has a commitment to the execution requests (I believe these are the withdrawals)? And the EL block validity guarantees the execution requests are valid for said block.

author: taulepton_
content: So a combination of commitments in the beacon block + payload validity

author: taulepton_
content: EL’s have been doing decoupled sync for the finalized prefix for a very long time

author: nero_eth
content: there are EL triggered exits which is an EL request but withdrawals go the other way (CL-> EL) whereas EL requests go EL -> CL

author: m.kalinin
content: CL knows the `F.parent_beacon_block_root` and that this root is valid according to its view which also commits to withdrawals of all ancestors of `F`. Then `F.parent_beacon_block_root` is a part of EL block header of the `F.payload` or the descendant payload if `F` is empty. If `block_hash` verification for `F.payload` passes it implies that EL payloads have the same content as CL blocks for the `[S, F]` range

author: taulepton_
content: Not sure on the specifics described here but if it is currently captured in one of the commitments in the engine API then using the checks we already do as described by <@425572898787426305> above I believe it should be covered. I’ll review the specs and a EL impl in around 30 to get more context.

author: m.kalinin
content:
This is where the payload is converted to block in geth: https://github.com/ethereum/go-ethereum/blob/master/beacon/engine/types.go#L306
Then the block hash is verified here: https://github.com/ethereum/go-ethereum/blob/master/beacon/engine/types.go#L255

author: nero_eth
content: Ah gotcha!

author: m.kalinin
content: looks like this simplifies a lot

author: nero_eth
content: If 8025 is "only" optional proofs, wouldn't all other nodes still need 8237?

author: m.kalinin
content: Nodes won't need 8237 to do the range sync post-Gloas as `parent_beacon_block_root` already an accumulator that we were looking for. Optional proofs doesn't change that

author: nero_eth
content:
Sorry, it's not clicking for me yet:
parent_beacon_block_root commits to e.g. state.latest_withdrawals_root in ssz, but no layer actually verifies that ssz_htr(payload.withdrawals) equals state.latest_withdrawals_root. CL doesn't have the payload during range sync, EL has no ssz, and the encodings don't match anyway (EL stores withdrawals as a keccak MPT root, BeaconState commits via SSZ htr). What am I missing here?

author: m.kalinin
content: Hmmm, `parent_beacon_block_root` of `F` will be verified but it's not necessarily that the grand parent beacon block root is correct on the EL side?

author: m.kalinin
content: Yeah, I see what you mean. I've got confused, need to think more into it

author: m.kalinin
content: The beacon block root isn't verified on the EL side. That's where the whole reasoning above breaks

author: m.kalinin
content: Historical payloads may have correct `parent_beacon_block_root` but at the same time the content of those payloads *may not* correspond to that root. The new accumulator solves this because it is verified on EL and CL side as opposed to the beacon block root which is only CL verifiable.

author: taulepton_
content: Can you elaborate?

author: taulepton_
content: for finalized prefix chain all beacon blocks are considered valid and final

author: m.kalinin
content: but we want to ensure that in the case if invalid block was finalized and there are 100% malicious nodes, a local node can detect that and reject the invalid chain

author: m.kalinin
content: `parent_beacon_block_root` commits to history `F` on the CL side. At the same time the payload that corresponds to the `F.payload_bid.block_hash` commits to the history `F'` -- we cannot verify that these two histories are equal with just the `block_hash` verification of the block `F`.

author: taulepton_
content: I believe that's correct, the EL works under the assumption that the finalized hash provided via fcu is valid and uses this as a trust anchor for decoupled historical sync.

author: m.kalinin
content: `block_hash` verification of `F.payload` implies `parent_beacon_block_root` in that payload is correct. It doesn't imply the `F.parent.parent_beacon_block_root` corresponds to the `F.parent.payload` and so force

author: taulepton_
content: isn't this the assumption we accept with weak-subjectivity?

author: m.kalinin
content: It's not just about EL trust assumptions, it's about the whole node to have the correct view on the chain even if an invalid block has been finalized. We don't want a node to be tricked into a situation where some malicious payload was finalized and the node can't recognize that and believes that its on the correct chain

author: taulepton_
content: Don't we already have this assumption about ancestry validity via weak subjectivity assumptions?

author: taulepton_
content: We assume that all historical blocks before the checkpoint are valid

author: m.kalinin
content: We assume that for a sync from scratch, a snap sync relies on the pivot block to be valid. But when a node went offline after it has been snap synced in the past, we want it to verify everything from the point of going offline. Imagine all honest experienced temporal outage, adversary can build a malicious block and then when honest back online they will accept that malicious block

author: taulepton_
content: Yeah I think if we want to be resistant to invalid finalized EL blocks then an accumulator makes sense.

author: taulepton_
content: I think currently this is handled in the CL. For example in lighthouse for finalized blocks it asserts consistency between withdrawals and blob commitments against the EL block txs.

author: m.kalinin
content: Some CL clients run `block_hash` validation and do not pass payloads to EL for the finalized prefix of the chain. But then the problem is that those CLs pull payloads from the network and EL does the same and we get a duplication in p2p

author: m.kalinin
content: Another reason for that EIP is CL's sync complexity post-Gloas, payloads aren't anymore inside of beacon block and there should be a separate by range request on CL side handling which causes a lot of complexity, I've heard this from Potuz at least
